title: ITLS Clinical Pearls & Study Hints
description: Key concepts, assessment tips, and clinical pearls from the International Trauma Life Support curriculum for exam preparation.

pearls.text: The ITLS Primary Survey follows C before A-B-C (Airway → Breathing → Circulation) — Airway is typically first in ITLS; however, life-threatening Catastrophic external hemorrhage may be addressed immediately if present, C before ABC.
The 'Golden Hour' concept: definitive care for life-threatening trauma must be reached quickly. Scene time should be limited to ≤10 minutes for critical patients (load-and-go).
ITLS Primary Survey: Scene size-up → Initial assessment → Rapid trauma survey → Vital signs → SAMPLE history. Identify and treat life threats as you find them.
ITLS Secondary Survey is only performed on stable patients or en route. It includes a detailed head-to-toe exam and focused history.
In trauma, use the modified jaw-thrust (not head-tilt/chin-lift) to open the airway while maintaining inline cervical spine stabilization.
Cervical spine immobilization is indicated for any significant mechanism of injury, neck pain, or altered mental status after trauma — even if the patient is ambulatory.
Tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation + hypotension + JVD. Treat immediately with needle decompression (2nd ICS, MCL). Do not wait for x-ray.
Open chest wounds ('sucking chest wounds') should be covered with a vented (3-sided) occlusive dressing to allow air to escape but prevent re-entry.
Flail chest: paradoxical movement of a chest wall segment. Usually associated with underlying pulmonary contusion. Treat with positive pressure ventilation if respiratory distress is present.
Hemorrhage control priority order: direct pressure → wound packing → tourniquet for extremity bleeding. Tourniquets should be placed 2–3 inches proximal to the wound.
Signs of hemorrhagic shock: tachycardia, pale/cool/clammy skin, decreased BP (late sign), decreased level of consciousness. Tachycardia is often the earliest sign.
Two large-bore IVs and fluid resuscitation are indicated for hemorrhagic shock. However, avoid aggressive over-resuscitation in penetrating trauma (permissive hypotension principle).
Shock = inadequate tissue perfusion. In trauma, the most common cause is hemorrhagic (hypovolemic) shock. Neurogenic shock (warm, bradycardic) suggests spinal cord injury.
Obstructive shock causes include tension pneumothorax and cardiac tamponade. Beck's Triad (JVD + muffled heart sounds + hypotension) points to tamponade — treat with pericardiocentesis.
Cushing's Triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations) is a late, ominous sign of increasing intracranial pressure (ICP). Treat with hyperventilation only if herniation is imminent.
Avoid hypoxia and hypotension in head-injured patients — both significantly worsen outcomes. Maintain SpO2 ≥94% and SBP ≥90 mmHg.

Spinal motion restriction is indicated when mechanism + symptoms or signs are present. A fully alert, unimpaired patient with no midline tenderness after low-energy mechanism may not require immobilization.
Complete vs. incomplete spinal cord injury: incomplete injuries (some function below the level) have a better prognosis. Priapism in a male trauma patient may indicate spinal cord injury.
Hemothorax: blood in the pleural space. Signs include decreased breath sounds + dullness to percussion on the affected side + shock. Large hemothorax requires chest tube drainage.
Cardiac contusion (myocardial contusion) should be suspected after sternal fracture or significant blunt chest trauma. Monitor for dysrhythmias.
Solid organ injuries (spleen, liver) bleed rapidly and cause hemorrhagic shock. Hollow organ injuries (bowel) cause peritonitis over hours. Both require surgical evaluation.
Seatbelt sign (abdominal ecchymosis from lap belt) suggests significant intraabdominal injury, even if the patient initially appears stable.
Femur fractures can cause 1–1.5 liters of occult blood loss. Traction splints (Hare, Sager, Kendrick) reduce pain and blood loss for mid-shaft femur fractures ONLY — not for hip or knee injuries.
The 6 P's of vascular/compartment compromise: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pressure. Compartment syndrome requires emergency fasciotomy.
Rule of Nines for adults: head = 9%, each arm = 9%, chest = 18%, back = 18%, each leg = 18%, perineum = 1%. Used to estimate total body surface area (TBSA) burned.

Stop the burning process first. Cool with room-temperature water (not ice). Remove jewelry and non-adherent clothing. Cover with dry sterile dressings. Initiate IV access and fluid resuscitation for major burns.

Children compensate well until decompensation is sudden. Tachycardia is an early warning sign of shock; hypotension is a late, pre-arrest finding. Normal BP in a child does NOT rule out significant hemorrhage.

Pediatric airway: proportionally larger tongue, more superior and anterior larynx, shorter trachea — all increase risk of obstruction and intubation difficulty. Use BVM ventilation as the primary approach.

Kinematics of trauma: understanding the energy transfer helps predict injury patterns. High-energy mechanisms (MVA, fall from height, GSW) require a thorough primary and secondary survey regardless of initial presentation.

Penetrating trauma: always identify entry and exit wounds (if present). Do NOT remove impaled objects in the field — stabilize them in place. Impaled objects in the chest/abdomen indicate immediate transport.
Ongoing assessment during transport should be performed every 5 minutes for critical patients and every 15 minutes for stable patients. Document all findings and changes.
Scene time ≤10 minutes for critical trauma (load-and-go). Perform critical interventions en route: IV access, splinting, further assessment. Do not delay transport for non-life-saving procedures.
Altered mental status (AMS) in a trauma patient is a red flag — consider hypoxia, hypotension, head injury, or hypoglycemia as causes. Always assess blood glucose.
Dark, venous blood oozes slowly and is controlled by direct pressure. Bright red, arterial blood spurts with the pulse and requires more aggressive hemorrhage control.
A combative or agitated trauma patient should be assumed to have hypoxia or hypoperfusion of the brain until proven otherwise — not simply a behavioral issue.
